figures.caption: Fig. 1 Cone-slice-ramp geometry with coordinate axes used in this work.
Table 3 Instrumentation locations (distances from origin) on the model with lines demarcating the sensors on the cone, slice, and ramp. • denotes corresponding measurements were made.

Fig. 2 Oil flow visualization for the cone-slice geometry (0◦ ramp) with a turbulent inflow (a) Mach 5, 𝑅𝑒 = 16.0 × 106 /m (b) Mach 8, 𝑅𝑒 = 16.2 × 106 /m. Cyan colored dashed curves highlight a few streaklines.

Fig. 3 Comparison of boundary-layer expansion for the cone-slice geometry (0◦ ramp) at Mach 5 and Mach 8. (a) Laminar case: Instantaneous image at Mach 5, 𝑅𝑒 = 3.6 × 106 /m with white dots marking the boundary layer edge for Mach 8, 𝑅𝑒 = 4.7 × 106 /m (b) Turbulent case: Instantaneous image at Mach 5, 𝑅𝑒 = 16.4 × 106 /m with white dots marking the boundary layer edge for Mach 8, 𝑅𝑒 = 16.2 × 106 /m

Fig. 4 Wall shear-stress measurements for the cone-slice geometry (0◦ ramp) (a) Mach 5, compared with turbulent and laminar correlations for a straight cone from [78] and [77], respectively (b) Comparison of high 𝑅𝑒 cases across Mach 5 (blue) and 8 (red). Mach 5 cases are 𝑅𝑒 > 16 × 106 /m and Mach 8 cases are 𝑅𝑒 > 14 × 106 /m. Measurements indicated with uncertainty bars represent variation across different runs. Correlations for a straight cone are also shown.

Fig. 5 PSD of pressure measured by PCBs at Mach 5 for cone-slice geometry (0◦ ramp) (a) Laminar case, 𝑅𝑒 = 8.6 × 106 /m, (b) Turbulent case, 𝑅𝑒 = 18.8 × 106 /m. Arrows show evolution of pressure-fluctuations and are discussed in text.

Fig. 6 Comparison of Turbulent PCB data at Mach 5 and Mach 8 for the cone-slice geometry (0◦ ramp) (a) Normalized PSD using outer scaling of the boundary layer on the cone, dashed lines are Mach 5 at 𝑅𝑒 = 18.8×106 /m and solid lines are Mach 8 at 𝑅𝑒 = 16.5×106 /m. (b) Normalized RMS Pressure computed in the two frequency bands bounded in (a) by dotted and dashed black lines.

Fig. 7 Temporally-averaged schlieren image at (a) Mach 5 and (b) Mach 8 for the cone-slice-ramp geometry with 30◦ ramp. 𝑅𝑒 values are = ×106 /m. Red dashed arrow annotates movement of the mean-separation location with Re. Annotations of SBLI features are described in the text.
Fig. 8 Surface streaklines from oil-flow for the cone-slice-ramp geometry with 30◦ ramp at (a) Mach 5 and (b) Mach 8. 𝑅𝑒 values are = ×106 /m.

Fig. 9 Heat flux derived from TSP for the cone-slice-ramp geometry with 30◦ ramp at (a) Mach 5 and (b) Mach 8. 𝑅𝑒 values are = ×106 /m. TSP data was unavailable in the region masked with a black rectangle. Annotations are discussed in the text.
Fig. 10 Instantaneous schlieren images in the transitional regime at Mach 5. 𝑅𝑒 values are in 106/m.
Fig. 11 Heat-flux coefficient at Mach 5 as measured using Schmidt-Boelter gauge for the cone-slice-ramp geometry with 30◦ ramp. The bottom subfigure shows the location of the sensors.

Fig. 12 Mean surface pressure along the plane of symmetry compared with inviscid estimates for the cone-sliceramp geometry with 30◦ ramp (a) Mach 5 (b) Mach 8, 𝑅𝑒 values are ×106 /m. CFD data at Mach 8 obtained from [59]

Fig. 13 PSD contours from combined Kulite (0.4− 50 kHz) and PCB (50− 500 kHz) data for laminar conditions, Mach 8, 𝑅𝑒 ≈ 4 × 106 /m, with a corresponding schlieren image (bottom). Dotted vertical lines show the sensor locations and horizontal dashed black lines demarcate the frequency ranges discussed in the text. A dashed red line annotates the shear-layer height and frequencies.
Fig. 14 Schlieren SPOD for Mach 8, 𝑅𝑒 = 4.3 × 106 /m. Dashed lines denote demarcations between frequency bands of analysis.

Fig. 15 (a) Evolution of mid-band peak frequency (f) obtained from Kulite PSDs and shear-layer height (h) obtained from averaged schlieren data. (b) Strouhal number evaluated using f, h, and edge-velocity. 𝑅𝑒 ≈ 4 × 106 /m for both Mach 5 and Mach 8 data.

Fig. 16 PSD near ramp corner for different ramp angles and Mach numbers (left) PSDs for 10◦ and 20◦ ramps have been shifted by 10−5 and 10−2, respectively. Schlieren SPOD modes at peak frequency for Mach 8 data (right). 𝑅𝑒 ≈ 4.0 × 106 /m

Fig. 17 Strouhal number comparison (a) Laminar regime for different ramp angles and Mach numbers, 𝑅𝑒 ≈ 4.0 × 106 /m, (b) Mach 8, 30◦ ramp with varying Re. Frequencies are taken for the sensor located at 𝑥 = −0.12

Fig. 18 PSD contours from combined Kulite (0.4 − 11 kHz) and PCB (11 − 500 kHz) data for transitional conditions. Mach 8, (a) 𝑅𝑒 ≈ 7.5×106 /m, (b) 𝑅𝑒 ≈ 9.4×106 /m with corresponding schlieren images. Annotations are discussed in the text.
Fig. 19 Schlieren SPOD for Mach 8, 𝑅𝑒 = 7.4 × 106 /m. Eigenmodes at three frequencies are shown.
Fig. 20 (a) Schlieren SPOD mode energy for Mach 8, 𝑅𝑒 = 9.3 × 106 /m. (b) Intermittent turbulent spots as observed in individual schlieren images, Mach 8, 𝑅𝑒 = 9.3 × 106 /m.

Fig. 21 (a) PSD contours from combined Kulite (0.4 − 11 kHz) and PCB (11 − 500 kHz) data for turbulent conditions at Mach 8, 𝑅𝑒 ≈ 16.5 × 106 /m with corresponding schlieren images. Annotations are discussed in the text. (b) Comparison of spectra on the slice, with and without the ramp.

Fig. 22 PSD contours at Mach 5 (left) and Mach 8 (right) from combined Kulite (0.4 − 50 kHz) and PCB (50 − 500 kHz) data at 𝑥 = −0.88 (a) and (b), −0.53 (c) and (d), and −0.11 (e) and (f). Dotted lines represent the 𝑅𝑒 tested. Annotations are discussed in the text.
